summary: I am originally from Ethiopia, where I grew up in the countryside, not in a city. In the 1970s, there was a war and political unrest, especially among students who protested against the government. Many people, including myself, had to leave Ethiopia because of the government's harsh response. My life there was simple, mostly supporting my family who were farmers.

After leaving Ethiopia due to the conflict, I went to Djibouti, a small country on the Mediterranean. I stayed there for about two years with a group of students who had also escaped the unrest. It was during this time that the United Nations opened an office there, which helped us register and seek opportunities to move elsewhere.

The United Nations played a crucial role in my journey after leaving Ethiopia. When they opened an office in Djibouti, I registered with them and was interviewed, which led to my opportunity to go to Egypt. Later, the UN office in Egypt helped coordinate applications for resettlement in Western countries like the United States.

I lived in Egypt for about five to six years after leaving Djibouti. During that time, I was supported by the United Nations office there, which helped me apply for resettlement in the West. I went to the American embassy in Egypt for an interview conducted in Arabic, which I passed despite not knowing English at the time.

I was fortunate to be accepted to come to the United States after my interview at the American embassy in Egypt. I arrived with no knowledge of English and settled in Minnesota. The U.S. became my new home where I have lived for over twenty years, building a life and raising my family.

Minnesota was my first state in the United States, and I have lived here for more than twenty years. I chose Minnesota because it felt welcoming and quiet compared to bigger cities. It became the place where I learned English, worked hard, and raised my family.

I have lived in St. Paul, Minnesota for almost twenty years. It is a quiet and friendly city where I feel I fit in well with the community. My sponsors met me at the airport here and helped me get started with shelter and food when I first arrived.

Compared to Minneapolis, I find St. Paul to be quieter and friendlier, which is why I prefer living here. Although I have not lived in Minneapolis, I often think about the differences between the two cities and appreciate the calmness of St. Paul.

When I arrived in Minnesota, I started learning English at the Hubbs Center. I had no knowledge of English at all, so this place was very important for me to begin my education and integration. I completed my English as a Second Language program there before moving on to further studies.

After finishing at the Hubbs Center, I attended St. Paul College for two years in the LPN program. It was very challenging balancing school, work, and family responsibilities. Eventually, I had to pause my studies to support my children, but I am proud that most of them are now in college.
